serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lamb pirattal is approximately 150g, equivalent to a moderate restaurant or hawker portion served as a side dish with rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Pirattal — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Pirattal (cr_id F06060009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb pirattal. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors being onion, tomato, and spices — all low-GI ingredients. Lamb itself has negligible GI impact. The composite dish GI is analogous to other low-carbohydrate Indian meat dishes (GI ~25–35) as referenced in Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller peer-reviewed GI tables. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 8.85) / 100 ≈ 2.7, where carbohydrate per 150g serving = 5.9 × 1.5 = 8.85g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1212mg per 100g, far exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit lamb pirattal due to its sodium content of 1212mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a 30–50g portion and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.9g/100g) with 2.9g dietary fibre supports glycaemic stability, but very high sodium (1212mg/100g) may indirectly affect metabolic health and blood pressure regulation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb pirattal is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–120g) due to its low carbohydrate and high protein content, but the high sodium load requires careful monitoring of total daily sodium intake and pairing with low-GI, low-sodium accompaniments such as cauliflower rice or whole-grain roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1212mg/100g) poses significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; lamb is also a source of saturated fat which may contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb pirattal to occasional small portions (50g or less) and prioritise home-prepared versions with significantly reduced salt, substituting lamb with leaner proteins such as chicken breast or fish to reduce saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways; sodium at 1212mg/100g substantially exceeds the 400mg/100g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid lamb pirattal due to its high arachidonic acid content and extremely high sodium; substituting lamb with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) and reducing salt significantly would substantially lower the inflammatory burden of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb pirattal is a South Indian and Mamak-style stir-fried lamb dish popular in Singapore's hawker centres and Indian Muslim (Mamak) restaurants. The dish consists of tender lamb pieces pre-marinated and then stir-fried at high heat with sautéed ginger, garlic, and green chilli, typically combined with onion, tomato, turmeric, and garam masala to produce a dry, intensely spiced coating. The texture is firm and slightly charred at the edges, with a bold, aromatic flavour profile dominated by warm spices, pungent alliums, and mild chilli heat. Pirattal (also spelled piratal or piratttal) derives from the Tamil word meaning 'to toss' or 'to stir', reflecting the high-heat dry-frying technique. Nutritionally, lamb pirattal is high in protein (16.1g/100g) and moderate in fat (7.6g/100g), with low carbohydrate (5.9g/100g) and meaningful dietary fibre (2.9g/100g). Its glycaemic index is estimated as low (~30), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in appropriate portions. However, the sodium content is extremely high at 1212mg/100g, posing significant risk for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice, roti canai, or naan, and is a staple of Mamak cuisine in Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is lamb pirattal suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06060009) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed analogous data as no direct GI measurement exists for this dish.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Mamak lamb pirattal preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06060009) or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. GI of approximately 30 is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein composition of the dish and peer-reviewed GI tables for comparable Indian meat dishes (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008). No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
